refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall establish and maintain teleoperation communication channels with end-to-end latency (sensor-to-operator-to-actuator) not exceeding 150 ms at the 95th percentile under agreed network conditions, enabling safe and responsive remote equipment control.
refined_fr.rationale: CR-NGHE-008 addresses a critical safety and operational requirement. Teleoperation latency directly impacts operator situational awareness and control responsiveness. The p95 ≤ 150 ms acceptance criterion is a hard constraint for safe remote operation. Refinement clarifies that latency is measured end-to-end (not just network transit), includes both uplink and downlink paths, and is conditioned on agreed network assumptions. High safety criticality demands explicit verification and traceability.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Measure end-to-end latency under representative network conditions (agreed bandwidth, jitter, packet loss profiles). Conduct operator acceptance testing with simulated remote tasks. Validate p95 latency across multiple network scenarios and load conditions. Establish continuous monitoring in field deployment.
sub_frs: FR-TEL-001: Acquire sensor data (position, orientation, video, environmental) at sufficient frequency to support 150 ms latency budget
FR-TEL-002: Transmit sensor telemetry to remote operator with bounded network latency
FR-TEL-003: Receive operator control commands at remote site with bounded network latency
FR-TEL-004: Execute received control commands on vehicle actuators with minimal processing delay
FR-TEL-005: Monitor and report teleoperation latency health and network conditions to operator and safety systems

candidate_dps: DP-A: Distributed real-time architecture with edge compute on vehicle, optimized sensor sampling, compressed telemetry streaming, and low-latency command execution pipeline
DP-B: Centralized cloud-based teleoperation with high-bandwidth network and aggressive caching/prediction to mask latency
DP-C: Hybrid edge-cloud with local vehicle autonomy fallback, selective telemetry compression, and adaptive latency compensation
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP-A: Distributed real-time architecture with edge compute on vehicle, optimized sensor sampling, compressed telemetry streaming, and low-latency command execution pipeline

potential_sources: Sensor sampling rate couples to network bandwidth and compression algorithm selection
Command execution latency couples to vehicle control system responsiveness and actuator characteristics
Network latency is independent variable; system design must accommodate agreed network conditions without requiring network infrastructure changes
coupling_check.notes: DP-A exhibits loose coupling between sensor acquisition, telemetry transport, and command execution. Each sub-function can be optimized independently within the latency budget. No circular dependencies detected. DP-B rejected due to tight coupling to cloud infrastructure and unpredictable latency. DP-C introduces unnecessary complexity and fallback logic that complicates verification.

assumptions_risks: ASSUMPTION: Network conditions (bandwidth, jitter, packet loss) are defined and stable within agreed parameters
ASSUMPTION: Vehicle edge compute platform has sufficient processing capacity for real-time sensor processing and telemetry compression
ASSUMPTION: Operator control interface can generate commands at sufficient frequency (≥10 Hz) to support responsive control
RISK: Sensor data acquisition and compression may introduce latency that consumes significant portion of 150 ms budget
RISK: Network variability (congestion, interference) may exceed agreed conditions, violating latency requirement
RISK: Operator reaction time and command generation latency not included in system latency budget; may require operator training/interface optimization
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Refine DP-A to explicitly allocate latency budget across sub-components: sensor acquisition (target ≤20 ms), telemetry compression/transmission (target ≤80 ms), command reception/execution (target ≤50 ms). Establish network SLA with agreed conditions and monitoring. Define fallback behavior if latency exceeds threshold.

im_boundary.name: Teleoperation
im_boundary.scope: Encapsulates all teleoperation-specific logic: sensor data acquisition and preprocessing, telemetry streaming, command reception and validation, actuator command execution, and latency monitoring. Owns the real-time processing pipeline and interfaces with vehicle control systems and network infrastructure.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Teleoperation module outputs sensor telemetry (video, position, orientation, environmental data) to remote operator via Network Link. Compressed and sampled to meet latency budget.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Teleoperation module receives operator control commands (movement, tool actuation, mode changes) from remote operator via Network Link.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Teleoperation module reports latency health and network condition alerts to Safety Services. Receives safety-critical alerts (e.g., emergency stop) from Safety Services.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Teleoperation module interfaces with network infrastructure for bidirectional communication with remote operator. Latency performance depends on network conditions.

module_containment_scores.notes: Teleoperation module is the natural and primary owner of all teleoperation-specific logic. Latency optimization, sensor acquisition, command execution, and network communication are core responsibilities. Minimal diffusion expected.

Vehicle module owns actuator control and low-level sensor interfaces, but teleoperation-specific processing (compression, latency optimization, remote command interpretation) should not be embedded in Vehicle. High diffusion risk if teleoperation logic scattered across vehicle subsystems.

Safety Services owns safety monitoring and alerts, but should not own teleoperation latency optimization logic. Latency monitoring is a safety concern but implementation belongs in Teleoperation module with alerts to Safety Services.
NGHE Solution is the top-level system container. Teleoperation is a major subsystem that should be owned by dedicated Teleoperation module, not diffused across solution-level logic.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The Teleoperation module is the appropriate owner for CR-NGHE-008 realization. It encapsulates the end-to-end latency optimization logic, sensor acquisition, telemetry streaming, command execution, and latency monitoring. External interfaces (Telemetry, Tasking, SafetyAlert, Network Link) are well-defined and minimal. Ownership is clear and diffusion risk is low.
containment_risks: RISK: If sensor acquisition logic is not fully owned by Teleoperation module (e.g., sensor drivers owned by Vehicle), latency optimization may be compromised. Mitigation: Define clear interface contract for sensor data delivery latency.

RISK: If network infrastructure is not under Teleoperation module control, latency SLA may be violated. Mitigation: Establish network SLA with agreed conditions and monitoring; Teleoperation module monitors and reports violations.
RISK: If actuator command execution latency is high (owned by Vehicle), overall latency budget may be exceeded. Mitigation: Allocate latency budget to Vehicle subsystem and verify compliance.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: Confirm that Teleoperation module owns sensor data acquisition pipeline (or has contractual latency guarantees from Vehicle module). Establish latency budget allocation across Teleoperation and Vehicle subsystems. Define network SLA and monitoring strategy. Clarify ownership of latency monitoring and reporting (Teleoperation with alerts to Safety Services).

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP-A (distributed real-time architecture) directly addresses FR requirement for end-to-end latency ≤150 ms. DP-A is feasible, testable, and aligns with safety-critical constraints. No uncoupled or circular dependencies detected. DP-A is superior to alternatives (DP-B cloud-dependent, DP-C overly complex).

diffusion_risks: Sensor acquisition latency must be contractually guaranteed by Vehicle module; if not, Teleoperation module may not achieve latency target
Network latency is external variable; Teleoperation module must monitor and report violations but cannot control network infrastructure
Actuator command execution latency must be within allocated budget; Vehicle module must verify compliance
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Teleoperation module is the clear owner of DP-A realization. Containment score 95/100 indicates strong fit. External interfaces (Telemetry, Tasking, SafetyAlert, Network Link) are well-defined and minimal. Ownership boundaries are clear. Diffusion risk is low if interface contracts are established with Vehicle and Safety Services modules.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: CR-NGHE-008 is verifiable through end-to-end latency measurement under agreed network conditions. Teleoperation module can be tested in isolation (with mocked network) and integrated with Vehicle and Safety Services. Acceptance criteria (p95 ≤ 150 ms) is measurable and objective.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Allocation of CR-NGHE-008 to Teleoperation module is clear. Sub-FRs (FR-TEL-001 through FR-TEL-005) are allocated to Teleoperation with interface contracts to Vehicle (sensor data, actuator control) and Safety Services (latency alerts). Latency budget allocation across sub-components is recommended but not blocking.

recommended_revisions: Establish latency budget allocation: sensor acquisition ≤20 ms, telemetry transmission ≤80 ms, command execution ≤50 ms. Verify feasibility with Vehicle and network teams.
Define network SLA with agreed conditions (bandwidth, jitter, packet loss) and establish monitoring/alerting in Teleoperation module.
Clarify interface contract between Teleoperation and Vehicle for sensor data delivery latency and actuator command execution latency.
Define fallback behavior if latency exceeds threshold (e.g., operator alert, autonomous safety mode, emergency stop).
Plan operator acceptance testing with simulated remote tasks to validate p95 latency under realistic conditions.

decision.justification: CR-NGHE-008 is a valid, high-priority requirement with clear DP and IM assignment. Teleoperation module is the appropriate owner. However, the requirement is not atomic and requires decomposition into sub-FRs (FR-TEL-001 through FR-TEL-005) to enable detailed design and verification. Latency budget allocation across sub-components must be established before detailed design. Recommend proceeding to next decomposition level for sub-FRs.
next_level_sub_frs: FR-TEL-001: Acquire sensor data (position, orientation, video, environmental) at sufficient frequency to support 150 ms latency budget
FR-TEL-002: Transmit sensor telemetry to remote operator with bounded network latency
FR-TEL-003: Receive operator control commands at remote site with bounded network latency
FR-TEL-004: Execute received control commands on vehicle actuators with minimal processing delay
FR-TEL-005: Monitor and report teleoperation latency health and network conditions to operator and safety systems

assumptions: Network conditions (bandwidth, jitter, packet loss) are defined and stable within agreed parameters
Vehicle edge compute platform has sufficient processing capacity for real-time sensor processing and telemetry compression
Operator control interface can generate commands at sufficient frequency (≥10 Hz) to support responsive control
Teleoperation module owns or has contractual latency guarantees for sensor data acquisition pipeline
Vehicle module owns actuator control and will meet allocated latency budget
Safety Services module will receive and act on latency health alerts from Teleoperation module
open_risks: Sensor data acquisition and compression may consume significant portion of 150 ms latency budget; feasibility not yet verified
Network variability (congestion, interference) may exceed agreed conditions, violating latency requirement
Operator reaction time and command generation latency not included in system latency budget; may require operator training/interface optimization
Actuator command execution latency may exceed allocated budget; Vehicle module compliance not yet verified
Fallback behavior if latency exceeds threshold not yet defined; safety implications unclear
evidence_needed: Latency budget allocation across sub-components (sensor acquisition, telemetry transmission, command execution) with feasibility analysis
Network SLA definition with agreed conditions and monitoring strategy
Interface contract between Teleoperation and Vehicle for sensor data delivery latency and actuator command execution latency
Operator acceptance testing results validating p95 latency under realistic conditions
Fallback behavior specification and safety analysis if latency exceeds threshold
Continuous latency monitoring and reporting strategy for field deployment
